APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper fits squarely within a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses sequence labeling and semi-supervised/multitask learning using a language-modeling auxiliary objective integrated into bidirectional LSTM architectures (with optional CRF and character components). The methods and evaluation meet venue expectations — experiments span 8 datasets (PTB, CoNLL-03, GENIA, CHEMDNER, JNLPBA, CoNLL-14, FCE, etc.), use standard metrics (entity-level F1, accuracy), compare to strong baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Lample et al. 2016), and report code and hyperparameters. The paper is aimed at the right audience and is well positioned within the NLP/ML research community; noted limitations (small POS gains, basic softmax LM) do not undermine its relevance. | B(5): This work addresses a core NLP/ML topic (sequence labeling) using semi-supervised/multitask learning and integrates a language-modeling auxiliary objective into widely used bidirectional LSTM/CRF architectures, which squarely fits top-tier NLP/ML venues. The paper evaluates across 8 established benchmarks (PTB, CoNLL-03, GENIA, JNLPBA, CHEMDNER, CoNLL-14, FCE) using standard metrics (F1, accuracy), compares to strong baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016), and provides code and hyperparameter details, matching community expectations. Given the relevance to both methodology and application, and its clear positioning within sequence-labeling and semi-supervised learning research, it is an excellent fit for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper fits squarely within a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses sequence labeling and semi-supervised/multitask learning using a language-modeling auxiliary objective integrated into bidirectional LSTM architectures (with optional CRF and character components). The paper is aimed at the right audience and is well positioned within the NLP/ML research community; noted limitations (small POS gains, basic softmax LM) do not undermine its relevance.

The paper evaluates across 8 established benchmarks (PTB, CoNLL-03, GENIA, JNLPBA, CHEMDNER, CoNLL-14, FCE) using standard metrics (F1, accuracy), compares to strong baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016), and provides code and hyperparameter details, matching community expectations. Given the relevance to both methodology and application, and its clear positioning within sequence-labeling and semi-supervised learning research, it is an excellent fit for a top conference.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important, practical problem—improving sequence labeling—by adding a lightweight language-modeling multitask objective that can be integrated into existing BiLSTM/CRF/character-level architectures. It shows consistent performance gains across eight diverse datasets (error detection, NER, chunking, POS) without requiring extra annotated or unannotated data, and provides code, increasing likelihood of adoption. While the gains on some tasks (POS) are small and the LM output is implemented with a basic softmax (limiting efficiency), the broad applicability and empirical improvements suggest a strong, likely-to-be-used contribution rather than a narrowly niche advance. | B(4): The paper addresses the broadly important task of sequence labeling by proposing a simple secondary language-modeling objective that predicts surrounding words and can be integrated into existing BiLSTM (+ optional CRF and character-level) architectures. It demonstrates consistent performance gains across eight diverse benchmarks (error detection, NER, chunking, POS) without requiring additional annotated or unannotated data and provides code, suggesting high practical adoptability. Improvements are limited on POS tagging where baselines are near upper bounds, so the contribution is strong and likely to be widely used but not fully transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem—improving sequence labeling—by adding a lightweight language-modeling multitask objective that can be integrated into existing BiLSTM/CRF/character-level architectures. While the gains on some tasks (POS) are small and the LM output is implemented with a basic softmax (limiting efficiency), the broad applicability and empirical improvements suggest a strong, likely-to-be-used contribution rather than a narrowly niche advance.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The summary indicates the paper is well organized and easy to follow (clear methodology description, datasets, and experiments) and the writing quality is explicitly noted as good. The main contributions are stated early (the added language-modeling objective and its integration with bidirectional LSTMs), and experimental reporting is thorough (8 datasets, metrics like entity-level F1 and accuracy, and hyperparameters are reported). Related work coverage is adequate and presentation appears consistent, though the summary does not detail figures/tables explicitly, so there may be minor presentation details to improve. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (introduction -> method -> experiments -> conclusion), with the main contributions stated early and a clear methodological description of augmenting BiLSTM sequence labeling with a language modeling objective. Notation appears consistent and the paper reports hyperparameters, provides code, and evaluates across 8 diverse datasets with standard metrics, suggesting effective use of figures/tables to present results. Writing quality is noted as good and related work coverage is adequate, though some minor presentation issues remain (e.g., brief discussion of limitations and efficiency concerns), so I rate it as well-written with only minor presentation issues.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes augmenting BiLSTM-based sequence labeling with a secondary language-modeling objective that predicts surrounding words—a practical multi-task/semi-supervised extension that integrates with CRF and character-level components. While the authors clearly differentiate from prior sequence-labeling baselines and show consistent gains across eight datasets without extra data, the core idea is largely an incremental combination of existing components (LM auxiliary loss + BiLSTM/CRF) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, yielding moderate novelty. | B(3): The core contribution—adding a token-level language modeling auxiliary objective to bidirectional LSTM sequence labeling models—is a concrete and useful combination but not a radical new idea. Sequence labeling itself is well-studied and the paper builds on existing architectures (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016), so the work reads as an incremental multitask extension rather than a fundamentally novel formulation. The clear differentiation (integration with CRF and char-LSTM, and consistent gains across eight datasets without extra data) makes it a non-trivial, practically informative contribution, but it is unlikely to surprise experts in the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes augmenting BiLSTM-based sequence labeling with a secondary language-modeling objective that predicts surrounding words—a practical multi-task/semi-supervised extension that integrates with CRF and character-level components. While the authors clearly differentiate from prior sequence-labeling baselines and show consistent gains across eight datasets without extra data, the core idea is largely an incremental combination of existing components (LM auxiliary loss + BiLSTM/CRF) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, yielding moderate novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The core contribution—adding a token-level language modeling auxiliary objective to bidirectional LSTM sequence labeling models—is a concrete and useful combination but not a radical new idea. Sequence labeling itself is well-studied and the paper builds on existing architectures (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016), so the work reads as an incremental multitask extension rather than a fundamentally novel formulation. The clear differentiation (integration with CRF and char-LSTM, and consistent gains across eight datasets without extra data) makes it a non-trivial, practically informative contribution, but it is unlikely to surprise experts in the field.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments across eight diverse sequence-labeling datasets and comparisons to strong baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016), with hyperparameters and code reported. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and none are necessary for the proposed multitask objective. Experimental methodology is generally sound (appropriate baselines, metrics, multiple tasks), though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing which is a minor omission. Overall the results justify the conclusions, with the paper honestly noting smaller gains on POS-tagging and efficiency/softmax limitations. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear empirically supported by experiments across eight diverse sequence-labeling datasets and comparisons to strong baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016). There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify and the proposed multi-task LM objective is straightforward and internally consistent. The experimental methodology is generally sound (appropriate baselines, metrics, hyperparameter reporting), though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or detailed ablations (a minor omission). Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, with only minor methodological reporting gaps that do not undermine the main claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments across eight diverse sequence-labeling datasets and comparisons to strong baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016), with hyperparameters and code reported. MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016) across a broad set of datasets (8 datasets including CoNLL-03, PTB, GENIA, etc.) using standard metrics (entity-level F1, accuracy), which indicates fair, consistent evaluation conditions. It acknowledges limitations where prior methods perform comparably (small POS gains) and reports code and hyperparameters, while related work coverage is described as adequate, so differences to baselines are discussed and the comparisons are comprehensive and fair. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Rei et al. 2016, Huang et al. 2015, Lample et al. 2016) across multiple standard datasets (CoNLL-03, PTB, GENIA, JNLPBA, etc.) using standard metrics (F1, accuracy), indicating fair comparison conditions. It explicitly acknowledges cases where gains are small (POS-tagging near upper bound) and reports hyperparameters and code, suggesting transparent and reproducible evaluation. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the methodological differences (adding a language-modeling auxiliary loss to bidirectional LSTM/CRF architectures) are clearly framed relative to prior approaches.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates the approach across a broad set of 8 datasets spanning error detection, NER, chunking and POS and compares to several strong baselines (Rei et al., Huang et al., Lample et al.), which supports the main claims. However the summary does not report explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contribution of the LM objective versus other components (CRF, character LSTM), a notable omission. Related work coverage is described as adequate, and limitations (small POS gains, basic softmax LM, training efficiency) are discussed; code and hyperparameters are provided, aiding reproducibility. Overall the experimental depth is strong but missing some targeted ablations/sensitivity checks, so I rate it a 4. | B(4): The paper evaluates the proposed LM auxiliary objective across a wide range of tasks and eight datasets (POS, NER, chunking, error detection) and compares to strong baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016), using appropriate metrics (F1, accuracy), which supports the central claims. It reports hyperparameters and provides code, and it explicitly discusses limitations (small POS gains, basic softmax LM and training-efficiency options), aiding reproducibility and transparency. However, the summary does not mention detailed ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the LM loss, architecture variants, or hyperparameter sensitivity, which would strengthen causal attribution of gains. Related work coverage is said to be adequate, so overall the experiments are solid with minor gaps in analysis depth.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the approach across a broad set of 8 datasets spanning error detection, NER, chunking and POS and compares to several strong baselines (Rei et al., Huang et al., Lample et al.), which supports the main claims. However the summary does not report explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contribution of the LM objective versus other components (CRF, character LSTM), a notable omission. Related work coverage is described as adequate, and limitations (small POS gains, basic softmax LM, training efficiency) are discussed; code and hyperparameters are provided, aiding reproducibility. Overall the experimental depth is strong but missing some targeted ablations/sensitivity checks, so I rate it a 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed LM auxiliary objective across a wide range of tasks and eight datasets (POS, NER, chunking, error detection) and compares to strong baselines (Rei et al. 2016; Huang et al. 2015; Lample et al. 2016), using appropriate metrics (F1, accuracy), which supports the central claims. It reports hyperparameters and provides code, and it explicitly discusses limitations (small POS gains, basic softmax LM and training-efficiency options), aiding reproducibility and transparency. However, the summary does not mention detailed ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the LM loss, architecture variants, or hyperparameter sensitivity, which would strengthen causal attribution of gains. Related work coverage is said to be adequate, so overall the experiments are solid with minor gaps in analysis depth.

main_claims: Proposes a sequence labeling framework that adds a secondary language modeling objective that learns to predict surrounding words for every word.

The language modeling objective can be integrated with bidirectional LSTM sequence labeling architectures (with optional CRF and character-level components) and is applicable to existing state-of-the-art frameworks.

The proposed LM objective provides consistent performance improvements across multiple sequence labeling benchmarks (8 datasets covering error detection, NER, chunking and POS-tagging) without requiring additional annotated or unannotated data.
_summary.methodology: Augment a bidirectional LSTM sequence labeling model (with optional CRF output and character-level LSTM representations) with a secondary training objective that predicts surrounding words for each token (a language modeling loss) to encourage learning of general-purpose semantic and syntactic features.
datasets: CoNLL 2014 shared task dataset (converted to error detection / CoNLL-14)
baselines: Rei et al. (2016) neural sequence labeling model
limitations: Improvements on POS-tagging are small because baseline performance is already close to the upper bound.
Current implementation uses a basic softmax for the language modeling output; training efficiency could be improved by methods such as noise-contrastive estimation or hierarchical softmax.
